content.full_text:
I 91-456

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 1991

TO: Members of the California Association of County Treasurers

FROM: Jeanne Pritchard, Division Chief
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division

RE: Follow-Up To Our Meeting On June 21, 1991, in Covina

I enjoyed meeting with you at the CACT conference in Covina.
Your input is extremely helpful in alerting us here at the
Commission to potential problem areas in applying the Political
Reform Act's provisions.

As I mentioned to you at the conference, the Commission's
primary job is to interpret the Act's complex provisions, put them
in language we can all understand, and assist those who have to
comply with the law. We spend most of our time and resources
trying to make the law easier to understand and comply with.

At the conference, you asked a number of questions for which
I did not have a thorough answer. I have researched those issues
and discussed them with our Legal Division attorneys. I hope the
following adequately responds to your questions.

As a reminder, the following monetary thresholds apply to the
Act's disclosure and disqualification provisions with regard to
gifts and honoraria, and to the new gift and honoraria limits:

Disclosure: Gifts and honoraria with an aggregate value of
$50 from a single source during a calendar year
must be disclosed.

Disqualification: Gifts and honoraria with an aggregate
value of $250 from a single source during a
12-month period trigger the disqualification
provisions.

Prohibition: ELECTED city and county officeholders are
prohibited from accepting gifts and honoraria
with an aggregate value of $1,000 from a single
source during a calendar year.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *
Important: The Commission is currently considering
regulations to interpret and apply the Act's provisions on the gift and honoraria limits. Therefore,
some of the information in this memorandum may change.
If you have not already done so, please request that
your name be placed on the mailing list for the FPPC
bulletin, which will carry information on the regulations.
* * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * *

I. The first series of questions has to do with transportation, meals, and accommodations received in a variety of
situations:

A. Received from a vendor (or any source other than the
county) in connection with attending equipment demonstrations or
other informational sessions:

Transportation, meals, and accommodations provided by a
vendor in connection with demonstrations or other informational
meetings or sessions are gifts and must be disclosed. The gift
limit applies unless specifically excluded under the Act. It does
not matter whether the payments are received as reimbursements to
you, or whether the travel expenses are prepaid.

B. Received from a vendor to attend training sessions on
equipment purchased by the county:

Travel payments received by county employees to attend
training on equipment may or may not be gifts to the employees.
If, for example, the contract for the purchase of the equipment
requires that the vendor provide travel, meals and accommodations
for such training, receipt of the travel, meals and accommodations
will not be a gift. On the other hand, absent such a contract,
the travel, meals and accommodations will be a gift.

In addition, the Commission, in its opinion In re Stone,
(1977) 3 FPPC 52, provided a limited exception to this rule in
cases where it is clear that the donor intended to make a gift to
a governmental entity, and not to a specific government official.
In the situation addressed in the Stone opinion, a city official
had received free transportation to deal with city business. The
Commission said that the free transportation would be deemed a
gift to the city, and not to the official, if all of the following
criteria were met:

1. The donor intended to donate the gift to the city and
not to the official;

-2-

2.  The city exercised substantial control over use of the
gift;

3.  The donor had not limited use of the gift to specified or
high level employees, but rather had made it generally
available to city personnel in connection with city business
without regard to official status; and

4.  The making and use of the gift was formalized in a
resolution of the city council (a written public record will
suffice for administrative agencies not possessing the
legislative power of adopting resolutions) which embodies
the standards set forth above.

Therefore, in the case of transportation, accommodations, and
meals received by a county treasurer, or an employee of the county
treasurer's office, if any of the above criteria is not met, the
official who receives the travel, accommodations, and meals must
report them as gifts from the vendor.  The gifts would be subject
to the gift limitation unless specifically excluded by the Act.

C.  Pursuant to the above rule set out in the Stone opinion,
with regard to transportation, accommodations, and meals, or other
gifts offered to the county treasurer's office, to the county
treasurer, or to an employee of the county treasurer's office, may
the county treasurer's office perform the steps required by the
Stone opinion, or must those steps be performed by the county
board of supervisors on behalf of the treasurer's office?

Gifts offered to the treasurer's office in accordance with
the Stone opinion must be gifts to the agency, and not gifts to
individuals.  Whether the procedure established by Stone may be
performed by the treasurer's office depends on whether the
treasurer's office is established as a separate agency or is
organized as a department or subunit of the county.  If the
treasurer's office is not a separate agency, the gift must be
offered to and accepted by the board of supervisors in order to
comply with the provisions of the Stone opinion.

In the near future the Commission will be considering a
regulation based on the Stone opinion.  Therefore, the advice
provided in this memorandum is subject to change.


-3-

D.   Received in connection with your attendance at an event
at which you gave a speech, participated on a panel or workshop,
or provided a similar service:

        Commission Regulation 18728 provides that the following
payments to you are neither gifts nor income and you are not
required to disclose them if you receive them in connection with
an event at which you give a speech of a substantive nature,
participate in a panel or seminar or perform a similar service:

    -- free admission, food, beverages and similar
       nominal benefits provided at the event

    -- reimbursement or advance for actual intrastate
       travel (travel outside California must be disclosed)

    -- reimbursement or advance for necessary accom-
       modations provided directly in connection with
       the event.

     In addition, such payments are not subject to the gift limit.

        This regulation will be reconsidered by the Commission in the
near future.  Please contact our office, or watch for a notice
about a revision to the regulation in the FPPC Bulletin.

     E.   Received from an association of which you are an
officer or member for attendance at a conference or other event at
which you served as the association's delegate or otherwise acted
as the representative of the association:

     If you are an officer or member of the association, and you
are representing the association at the meeting or conference, the
travel costs and other payments received from the association are
income, rather than gifts, and are not subject to the gift
limitation.  If you receive payments totaling $250 or more during
a calendar year, you must disclose the income on Schedule D of
your statement of economic interests.

     II.  When certain costs of attending events sponsored by an
organization of which an official is a member are paid for by
vendors, has the official received a gift from the organization,
or from the vendors?

     This question has been addressed in the Brockman Advice
Letter, No. I-91-96, attached.


                                    -4-

III. Association membership: are dues paid by vendors (or
other members which are not public officials) which may be used to
help pay for association events "gifts" to the officials who
attend the events?

     See the Brockman Advice Letter, No. I-91-96, attached.

IV. Raffle Prizes; Awards: A variety of questions arose with
regard to whether an official has received a gift or income when
he or she wins a raffle prize, a door prize, or a prize for
participating in a competition, such as a golf tournament:

     A prize or award won by an official in a bona fide
competition not related to the filer's official status may be
reported as income and is not subject to the gift limit. However,
income of $250 or more from a single source in a calendar year
must be disclosed.

     If the participants in a raffle or competition are primarily
government officials, a prize or award won by an official may be
related to official status and, therefore, be deemed a gift
subject to the gift limit. However, each competition must be
evaluated on a case-by-case basis. Gifts of $50 or more in a
calendar year must be disclosed.

     Following are specific situations you asked about:

     A. State lottery prize:

     Participation in the state lottery is a "bona fide
competition not related to the filer's official status." A
lottery prize is considered income, and must be disclosed if $250
or more is won during a calendar year. Lottery winnings are not
subject to the gift limit.

     B. Raffle prize, or award received for winning a golf
tournament or other competition, if the raffle or competition is
not open to the general public -- that is, the participants
primarily are government officials or employees:

     Such a prize or award must be disclosed as a gift if the
value is $50 or more. The prize or award is subject to the Act's
gift limit.




                                    -5-

C. Raffle prize, or award received for winning a golf
tournament or other competition, if the raffle or competition is
open to the general public -- that is, the event is not
principally for government officials:

Such a prize or award is income and must be disclosed if the
value is $250 or more. It is not subject to the Act's gift limit.


X. Discussion of penalties in connection with violation of
the new gift and honoraria limitations:

The Fair Political Practices Commission can bring an
administrative action against an official who violated the
Political Reform Act, and can impose administrative penalties of
up to $2,000 for each violation. An official who violates the Act
may be subject to a civil lawsuit, in which a court may impose a
fine. A willful violation of the Act is also a misdemeanor,
punishable by a fine up to $10,000 or by imprisonment, and may
result in the official being ineligible to run for public office
for four years. The Attorney General and district attorneys have
enforcement responsibilities with regard to criminal violations.




PLEASE KEEP IN MIND THAT THE INFORMATION IN
THIS MEMO RESPONDS ONLY TO THE SPECIFIC
QUESTIONS YOU ASKED. IT DOES NOT PROVIDE
A COMPREHENSIVE EXPLANATION OF THE
POLITICAL REFORM ACT'S PROVISIONS.




-6-
